3A_Bibliographic.citation: Nagata, J. M., Palar, K., Gooding, H. C., Garber, A. K., Bibbins-Domingo, K., & Baker, F. C. (2022). Associations between adverse childhood experiences, adolescent screen time and physical activity during the COVID-19 pandemic. Academic Pediatrics, 22(8), 1362-1370.

3B_Sample_Data.verbatim_sample_description: We used data from the from the 3.0 data release which included baseline (2016-2018) one-year follow-up (2017-2019), the first half of 2-year follow-up (2018-2019), and the COVID Rapid Response Research (RRR) Survey 1 (May 2020). We omitted study participants who were missing data for sociodemographic variables or did not complete the COVID RRR Survey 1. After omitting these participants, the sample consisted of 6749 adolescents predominately aged 12-13 (range 10-14)
3B_Sample_Data.notes: ACE exposure from baseline/1yr/2yr; screen time and physical activity from COVID May 2020 survey.

3C_Exposure.exposure_instrument: ABCD Study ACE items based on CDC-Kaiser ACE Study (9 of 10 ACEs; emotional abuse not assessed)
3C_Exposure.modalities: N/A - ACEs are the exposure, not screen time
3C_Exposure.verbatim_exposure_description: ACE score was determined through parent and adolescent responses in the baseline (2016-2018), 1-year follow-up (2017-2019), and first half of 2-year follow-up (2018-2019) surveys.
3C_Exposure.notes: IMPORTANT: In this paper, screen time is the OUTCOME, not the exposure. ACEs predict screen time.

3D_Outcome.primary_outcome: Screen time (hours/day), physical activity (hours/week), and days completing 60 min exercise per week during COVID-19 (May 2020)
3D_Outcome.outcome_instrument: COVID RRR Survey 1 screen time items (7 modalities summed); adapted IPAQ Short-form for physical activity
3D_Outcome.verbatim_outcome_description: Adolescents self-reported hours of use on a typical day of the following 7 modalities: multiple-player gaming, single-player gaming, texting, social media, video chatting, browsing the internet, and watching or streaming movies, videos, or television shows, excluding schoolwork. Total screen time was calculated by summing average screen time of these seven modalities.
3D_Outcome.notes: Screen time is the outcome here, predicted by ACEs.

3E_Design_Analysis.model_type_detail: Weighted linear regression with propensity weights from ABCD Study based on American Community Survey
3E_Design_Analysis.covariates_listed: age, sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parent education, site, school closure, child diagnosed with COVID-19, loss of household income due to COVID-19
3E_Design_Analysis.verbatim_analysis_description: Linear regression analyses estimated associations between reported ACE score (0-9, continuous) and May 2020 screen time and physical activity. Analyses were adjusted for sociodemographic characteristics and COVID-19 related variables listed above.

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Hours per day of screen time (May 2020, adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours per day of screen time per 1-unit increase in ACE score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Adjusted Model: ACEs 0.44 (0.26, 0.62) <.001 [Hours per Day of Screen Time]

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Hours per week of physical activity (May 2020, adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours per week of physical activity per 1-unit increase in ACE score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Adjusted Model: ACEs -0.17 (-0.34, 0.00) .048 [Hours per Week of Physical Activity]

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Days completing at least 60 min of exercise in past week (May 2020, adjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Days per week per 1-unit increase in ACE score
3F_Effect_Sizes.verbatim_quote: Adjusted Model: ACEs -0.09 (-0.14, -0.03) 0.001 [Days Completing at Least 60 Min of Exercise in the Past Week]

3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome: Hours per day of screen time (May 2020, unadjusted)
3F_Effect_Sizes.outcome_scale_note: Hours per day of screen time per 1-unit increase in ACE score

3G_Reporting_Quality.abstract_significance_verbatim: a higher ACE score was significantly associated with greater hours per day of screen time
3G_Reporting_Quality.notes: The paper reports that youth with 4+ ACEs had 2.3 more hours of screen time per day, which the authors characterize as meaningful. The per-ACE-unit effect is 0.44 hours/day (about 26 minutes per additional ACE). Effect sizes for physical activity are smaller.

3H_Recommendations.recommendation_verbatim: Clinicians caring for youth exposed to trauma in the postpandemic environment should explore screen time and physical activity behaviors.

3I_Rhetorical_Moves.modifiable_factor_verbatim: clinicians and future programming caring for ACE-exposed adolescents and their families can provide education about 1) the potential harms of heavy digital use and 2) the biological and psychosocial benefits of physical activity to reduce stress
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.per_unit_verbatim: youth with >=4 ACEs averaged 2.3 additional hours of screen time per day, 0.8 fewer hours of physical activity per week, and 0.5 fewer days of 60 minutes of exercise per week
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.discussion_characterization: The discussion frames ACEs as a risk factor for higher screen time and lower physical activity during the COVID-19 pandemic. It emphasizes that the 2.3-hour difference in screen time for high-ACE youth represents a meaningful behavioral difference. The paper recommends screening for ACEs and educating families about screen time harms and physical activity benefits, though the study design (cross-sectional within the COVID survey) limits causal claims.
3I_Rhetorical_Moves.ambiguity_notes: Screen time is the outcome, not the exposure. This is an ACE-to-screen-time study, not a screen-time-to-health-outcome study.
